summary: Yegge defends his notoriously long blog posts against critics who want shorter entries. He argues that his posts are intentionally essay-length (around 10 pages) because longer content has better 'survival characteristics' - greater reach and lasting impact. He presents a 'brain-cache theory' suggesting the ideal essay length should fill a reader's mental short-term cache and force ideas to be committed to long-term memory. This requires roughly one 'sitting' of 20-50 minutes, which is why his posts are calibrated to be exhausting but not crushing.
key_points: Blog length and popularity have a causative relationship - bigger entries create bigger splashes, though there's an upper limit before readers drop off
The ideal essay length is measured in duration (one sitting of ~50 minutes max), not word count, because the goal is to fill the reader's mental cache
Essays aim to 'imprint' readers with ideas by forcing content from short-term memory into long-term storage through sustained engagement
Different readers will remember different parts of an essay due to randomness in how our brains discard information, so good essays say the same thing multiple ways
Blogs are the best available 'turn-key essay publishing system' even though readers arrive expecting cat pictures instead of 10-page arguments
money_quotes: It's as if you're all in a pond, and I'm throwing a rock into it. Bigger rock, bigger splash.
If you want to make a lasting impression, then you need to fill up the reader's second-level cache and start blowing pages out into their long-term memory.
It's one of the amazing miracles of the internet: write-only people. They can't read but they somehow find a way to write.
The right length for an essay, I believe, is exactly 'one sitting': no more, no less.
If I throw in a sufficiently large rock, it'll crush you, which for most of you is an undesirable outcome.
My blogs may or may not be any good, but they're widely read. So are they really too long?
By getting you to disagree with it, I've left my mark. At least you'll remember the idea now.
key_insight: Long-form writing succeeds not despite reader fatigue but because of it - exhausting the reader's mental short-term cache forces ideas into permanent memory.
